engineering.translation: There is a specific kind of pain Krishna names here as the sharpest version of the disgrace argument. The peers who once respected your earned prowess — through migrations executed cleanly, incidents handled without blame, hard schema choices that survived three product cycles — those colleagues are not the threat. The threat is engineers who already wished you would fail: the bypassed reviewer, the architect whose design you replaced, the senior whose roadmap you rerouted. Watching you abandon a migration mid-flight, those people will not say 'they had reasons' in private. They will say 'they could not finish what they started,' in the only forum where rewriting lands — Slack, the architecture review channel, the post-mortem. Indecent words about your capability, spoken by enemies in the assembly of warriors. The verse claims there is no greater pain than this, for someone whose identity is the work.
engineering.concrete_scenario: It is week three of a Postgres-to-Aurora migration that has visibly slipped, and the senior engineer leading it has begun privately drafting a memo titled 'Pausing the Migration: Lessons.' The memo is well-reasoned. The risk register is honest. There are real arguments in it. What the memo does not name: the principal whose original Postgres design this migration was retiring, who has been quiet on the architecture review thread but has been forwarding slip-summary screenshots to two other principals. There is also the staff engineer the senior bypassed when designing the migration plan, who watched the bypass and remembered. If the project pauses now, the memo will circulate; the senior will not be the one circulating it. By the next architecture review, two principals will have read a version of events in which the migration failed because the senior could not handle Aurora's connection-pooling model — a version that will be partially true and entirely framed by people who already wished the project would fail. The senior's earned reputation — three previous migrations that landed clean, two production-grade Datadog dashboards now in widespread use — will be reread retrospectively as 'lucky.'
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses it to override genuinely correct decisions to pause or kill projects under social pressure. Not every project should be finished; some should be killed, and the killing is the right call even when enemies will rewrite the kill as failure. The verse identifies a specific failure mode (retreat under social-cost pressure when the technical case for completion is sound) — it does not authorize finishing every project to deny critics their narrative.
engineering.counter_example: When the senior engineer's enemies are RIGHT — when the migration genuinely cannot land, when the technical reading from outside the project is more honest than the reading from inside — the disgrace argument inverts. Krishna in 2.36 is not telling Arjuna 'fight because critics will mock you'; he is saying 'fight because the technical case for fighting is sound, AND retreat will additionally produce this pain.' The verse layers a social cost onto a sound technical conclusion; it does not invent a technical conclusion from a social cost. Chapter 18.66's right-renunciation is the corrective.
engineering.implication: Before pausing a project that has visibly slipped, name explicitly which colleagues will narrate the pause, in which forums, with which framings — and weigh that against the technical case for completion.
engineering.quotable_line: The peers who already wished you would fail will rewrite the slip as inability — in the architecture review channel, where the rewriting lands.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.36 is the third and sharpest verse in the disgrace-argument sequence (2.34-2.36). Engineering layer maps Krishna's three intensifying claims (peers will speak indecent words; in the only forum where the rewriting has weight; about the prowess you genuinely earned) onto the senior engineer's specific professional vulnerability. Concrete scenario uses a Postgres-to-Aurora migration mid-slip with named role types (principal, staff engineer) to keep the pain specific to engineering. Counter-example explicitly anchors to Ch. 18.66 to prevent the verse becoming social-pressure cover. Tags from controlled vocabulary: engineer-paralysis, duty, team-pathology.

v1: trimmed total word count from 651 to under 600 (concrete_scenario tightened, implication shortened) and lifted lexical diversity from 0.517 to ≥0.55 by replacing repeated terms with synonyms ('project' for one of three repetitions of 'migration'; 'retrospectively' / 'now in widespread use' added richer vocabulary). Quotable_line rewritten to be more specific ('already wished you would fail' instead of 'once respected'). Translation tightened to remove the duplicate 'engineer' / 'engineering' instances and the redundant 'on-call rotation' clause.
